titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Edgeworth to Frances Anne Edgeworth, May 3, 1819
titleStmt.author: Maria Edgeworth
titleStmt.sponsor: Wake Forest University, University of Tennessee, Xavier University, Texas A&M University
titleStmt.principal: Susan Egenolf, Meredith Hale, Hilary Havens, Jessica Richard, and Robin Runia

editionStmt.edition: First digital edition in TEI, date:

availability.licence: Distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Unported License
availability.p: Reproduced by courtesy of Bodleian Library
availability.licence: Distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Unported License

msDesc.head:
Letter from
Maria Edgeworth 1768-1849 71477273 62567029
to Frances Anne Edgeworth,

editorialDecl.p: Edgeworth’s spelling and punctuation are retained in transcription, including non-standard spelling, line breaks, and hyphenation of words. The long s and ligatured forms are silently emended.

dateline.date: May 3[superscript]d[/superscript] 1819 12 o clock P.M. Kensington Gore

div.p:
194 Now that every thing is packed up &
ready to put into the carriage I may in..
dulge myself in scribbling a few lines
to my dear mother

Lotte dear Francis is with us & we are
all going together to High Wycombe - a fine
day - the carriage will be open - for the first
7 or 8 miles Fanny will ride with M[superscript]r[/superscript]
S Whitbread and F will be in the open
carriage between her and I - delighted we are
to have him - after wards George will smug..
gle him up in the seat behind & we can
talk to him over the head. NB two grown
up people have sat in it before therefore do
not imagine it will not hold him

Lady E W just now took me down to her
own room to ask me whether she might
have Francis here at any time when we
are gone. " Whenever it should happen to
be inconvenient to Capt[superscript]n[/superscript]. B or his other
relations to have him remember I shall
like to have him here - Before I had seen
him I would not have asked any school
boy to stay in my family I sh.[superscript]d[/superscript] have feared
the charge. But he is such an amiable
amiable creature as well as so intelligent
and so able to occupy & to take care of him
self that it will not be a charge but a pleasure
to me to have him at any time & for any time

I thanked for you with all the warmth
with which I know you will feel this -
Lady E W is exceedingly fond both of Fanny
& Honora & is certainly sorry to part with
us. She does not absolutely refuse to come
to see us in Ireland tho she says she is
not yet equal to it . Miss Grant wishes it
for Lady E as well as for herself - and her
youngest son M[superscript]r[/superscript]. SW would delight in it - As I am
in the habit of inviting people to other peo-
ple's houses I must say that I wish Lovell
& you would write to Lady E W expressing
a desire to see her & Miss Grant & M[superscript]r[/superscript]. S
Either of her sons who could accompany
her. - I know she would be gratified . We
read to her your account of Lovell's sherif
=alty - the jail - the Judge's just compli=
ment praise of the High Sheriff of the C[superscript]y[/superscript] of
Longford . She was much pleased and
from this & various circumstances has
a great desire to know Lovell - You she
loves from seeing you reflected in your
child & your child in law & your brother
& little son. She says she thinks she could
be more at ease at Edgeworths Town than any
where but in her own home & family

195 When M[superscript]r[/superscript] Carr was telling a story of a dog he asked F. if he knew
(A) ^what was meant by instinct? = F[superscript]s[/superscript] answered
I mean a power which animals possess not
derived from experience reason nor to be im
proved by experience

I do not think Dugald Stewart or Newton Locke c[superscript]d[/superscript] have
worded it more exactly - and M[superscript]r[/superscript]. M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. & Miss Carr
aver that this is word for word his answer.
no himself

Many answers which Francis
makes to the Carr's Markets &c when they
ask him questions! M[superscript]r[/superscript]. Carr told him a curious
story of a dog - I said, I think this is more
than we c[superscript]d[/superscript]. call instinct. = What do you
(A)
